food_subgroup: Leafy green vegetable dish with mushroom, stir-fried

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a spinach entry but no specific stir-fried spinach with mushroom composite dish entry. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers spinach as a raw commodity. No composite stir-fried spinach-mushroom dish code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 256319004 covers spinach as a substance. No specific prepared dish code exists; closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical shared serving of stir-fried spinach with mushroom at a Chinese restaurant is approximately 200 g, representing one side dish shared among 2–3 diners.

nutrient_source.title: Spinach, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 11458) and Mushrooms, white, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 11260); composite estimate weighted 75% spinach, 25% mushroom with adjustment for stir-fry oil addition (~5 g vegetable oil per 100 g dish)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried spinach with mushroom exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked spinach (NDB 11458) and cooked white mushroom (NDB 11260) at a 75:25 ratio by weight, with an additional 5 g vegetable oil per 100 g dish to account for stir-frying. Oyster sauce contribution to sodium estimated at approximately 80 mg per 100 g dish. All values are approximate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; non-starchy leafy vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried spinach with mushroom. GI estimated at 15 based on non-starchy leafy green vegetables and mushrooms, which are consistently reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) as having GI values in the range of 10–20. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 9.2) / 100 = 1.4, where serving carbohydrate = 4.6 g per 100 g × 200 g serving = 9.2 g.

hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g serving provides approximately 460 mg sodium, primarily from oyster sauce; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 1.4 per serving, stir-fried spinach with mushroom has a negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable as a regular vegetable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High potassium, folate, and vitamin K with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides potassium (~840 mg per serving), folate (~260 mcg per serving), and vitamin A with minimal saturated fat (1.2 g per serving), supporting vascular health; however, sodium from oyster sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Spinach with Mushroom (鲜菇菠菜, Xiān gū bōcài) is a classic Chinese vegetable side dish consisting of fresh spinach and mushrooms rapidly cooked in a wok over high heat with garlic, vegetable oil, and oyster sauce. The dish is a staple of Cantonese and Chinese home cooking as well as restaurant menus across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The cooking method — high-heat wok stir-frying — preserves the vibrant green colour of the spinach while imparting a light smoky wok-hei flavour. The texture is tender-wilted spinach with slightly firmer mushroom pieces, and the taste is savoury, mildly garlicky, and umami-rich from the oyster sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g) and rich in micronutrients including vitamin A (310 mcg RAE), vitamin C (18 mg), folate (130 mcg DFE), iron (2.6 mg), calcium (110 mg), potassium (420 mg), and magnesium (58 mg) per 100 g. The fat content is moderate due to stir-fry oil but saturated fat is low. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making this dish highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary clinical concern is sodium from oyster sauce (approximately 230 mg per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and dairy-free. It is commonly served as a shared side dish in Chinese family-style dining and is widely available at zi char (煮炒) stalls and Chinese seafood restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Calories in stir-fried spinach with mushroom

Sodium content of Chinese spinach dish
What is xian gu bo cai?

Can I eat spinach with mushroom if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: Healthy vegetable option at Chinese seafood restaurants
Low-calorie side dish for weight management

confidence_reason: No exact entry for stir-fried spinach with mushroom as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked spinach and cooked mushroom components at a 75:25 ratio with stir-fry oil addition. GI is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked spinach (NDB 11458) and cooked white mushroom (NDB 11260) at a 75:25 weight ratio, with approximately 5 g vegetable oil per 100 g dish added to account for stir-frying, and approximately 80 mg sodium per 100 g added to account for oyster sauce. These are approximations; actual restaurant values may vary. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products other than oyster sauce; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods and confirmed negligible in this preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as vegetable oils used in stir-frying in Singapore are predominantly palm olein or soybean oil with negligible industrial trans fat content; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy oil entries. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the dish contains no animal flesh or eggs; confirmed zero for plant-based stir-fried vegetables per USDA SR Legacy. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as vitamin D is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spinach or cooked mushrooms used as the basis for this estimate; note that some mushroom varieties exposed to UV light may contain vitamin D but this is not confirmed for the specific mushroom type used. 6. GI is set to 15 as an analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy green vegetables and mushrooms per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). 7. The restaurant serving this dish (The Seafood Market by House of White Bee Hoon) is a Chinese seafood restaurant; the dish is a standard Chinese vegetable side. 8. Allergen note: oyster sauce contains mollusc-derived ingredients and is not suitable for individuals with shellfish/mollusc allergies. The dish is not vegan due to oyster sauce.
